title: Samuel Lyde

infobox.value: (1825–1860) was an [English](/wiki/English_people "English people") writer and [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") missionary who lived and worked in [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") in the 1850s and wrote a pioneering book on the [Alawite](/wiki/Alawite "Alawite") sect. In 1856, he sparked months of anti-Christian rioting in [Ottoman Palestine](/wiki/History_of_Palestine#Ottoman_period "History of Palestine") when, during a visit there, he killed a beggar.

Robert James Dodds and Joseph Beattie had begun their missionary work in Syria in 1856 on behalf of a Reformed Presbyterian denomination known as the Reformed Presbyterian Church Old Light Synod.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

"The Rediscovery of Samuel Lyde's Lost Nusayrī Kitāb al-Mashyakha (Manual for Shaykhs)". *[Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society](/wiki/Journal_of_the_Royal_Asiatic_Society "Journal of the Royal Asiatic Society")*. **24**: 1–16 (page 5). Accademia nazionale dei Lincei, Fondazione Leone Caetani. pp. 149–169, (p.158). [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-88-218-0437-3](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-88-218-0437-3 "Special:BookSources/978-88-218-0437-3"). Retrieved 9 January 2013.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British missionary and author (1825–1860)

**Samuel Lyde** (1825–1860) was an [English](/wiki/English_people "English people") writer and [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") missionary who lived and worked in [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") in the 1850s and wrote a pioneering book on the [Alawite](/wiki/Alawite "Alawite") sect. In 1856, he sparked months of anti-Christian rioting in [Ottoman Palestine](/wiki/History_of_Palestine#Ottoman_period "History of Palestine") when, during a visit there, he killed a beggar.

## Life and missionary work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samuel_Lyde&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and missionary work")]

[![Samuel Lyde is located in Eastern Mediterranean](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Mediterranean_Sea_East_location_map.svg/250px-Mediterranean_Sea_East_location_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Mediterranean_Sea_East_location_map.svg "Samuel Lyde is located in Eastern Mediterranean")

![Bhamra](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Red_pog.svg/20px-Red_pog.svg.png)

Bhamra

![Nablus](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Red_pog.svg/20px-Red_pog.svg.png)

Nablus

![Alexandria](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Red_pog.svg/20px-Red_pog.svg.png)

Alexandria

![Beirut](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Red_pog.svg/20px-Red_pog.svg.png)

Beirut

![Cairo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Red_pog.svg/20px-Red_pog.svg.png)

Cairo

Modern Syria and surrounding countries, showing locations visited by Lyde including the village of Bhamra where he established his mission.

Lyde was born in 1825.[[1]](#cite_note-Lindemann-1) He obtained a degree in 1848 after studying at [Jesus College, Cambridge](/wiki/Jesus_College,_Cambridge "Jesus College, Cambridge") and in 1851 he was awarded an [M.A](/wiki/Master_of_Arts_(Oxbridge_and_Dublin) "Master of Arts (Oxbridge and Dublin)"), took [holy orders](/wiki/Holy_orders "Holy orders") as a [clergyman](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy") of the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and became employed as a [fellow](/wiki/Fellow "Fellow") of Jesus College.[[2]](#cite_note-Krieger5-2) Poor health, according to Lyde, prevented him from "exercising the duties of his profession in England, at least during the winter months" and, therefore, in the winter of 1850/1851 he made "the usual tour" of [Egypt](/wiki/Khedivate_of_Egypt "Khedivate of Egypt") and [Syria](/wiki/Ottoman_Syria "Ottoman Syria").[[3]](#cite_note-lyde-3) While on the "tour", he decided, because of his health, to settle permanently in Syria, then a part of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire").[[3]](#cite_note-lyde-3) While visiting [Beirut](/wiki/Beirut "Beirut"), the British [consul](/wiki/Consul_(representative) "Consul (representative)") suggested to him that he could occupy his time by working as a missionary to the [Alawites](/wiki/Alawites "Alawites"),[[3]](#cite_note-lyde-3) also known as Nusayris, a secretive mountain sect who later provided two of modern Syria's leaders: [Bashar al-Assad](/wiki/Bashar_al-Assad "Bashar al-Assad") and his father, [Hafez al-Assad](/wiki/Hafez_al-Assad "Hafez al-Assad").[[4]](#cite_note-Telegraph-4)

Lyde was persuaded by the idea. From 1853 to 1859, he lived among the Alawite [Kalbiyya](/wiki/Kalbiyya "Kalbiyya") community, and established a mission and school in [Bhamra](/wiki/Bhamra,_Syria "Bhamra, Syria"),[[5]](#cite_note-Moosa-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) a village overlooking the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") port of [Latakia](/wiki/Latakia "Latakia").[[7]](#cite_note-gua05-7) However, he later wrote that living among them convinced him that the Alawites fulfilled [St Paul](/wiki/St_Paul "St Paul")'s description of the heathen: "filled with all unrighteousness, fornication, wickedness, covetousness, maliciousness".[[4]](#cite_note-Telegraph-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Nablus_1898.jpg/250px-Nablus_1898.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nablus_1898.jpg)

Nablus at the end of the 19th century

Lyde travelled to [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)") in 1856, and as he rode on his horse into [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus") he shot and killed a beggar who was trying to steal his coat.[[8]](#cite_note-Tibawi-8)[[9]](#cite_note-figes-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Ininopulos-10) It was either an accidental discharge of the gun or Lyde had lost his nerve and fired.[[8]](#cite_note-Tibawi-8) An anti-[Christian](/wiki/Christians "Christians") riot ensued during which Christian houses were burned and several [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") and [Prussians](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") were killed.[[9]](#cite_note-figes-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Ininopulos-10) Lyde took refuge in the town governor's house but was eventually put on trial for murder.[[9]](#cite_note-figes-9) The only witnesses were three women who accused him of attacking and deliberately killing the beggar.[[9]](#cite_note-figes-9) However, the testimony of women was inadmissible in [Ottoman courts](/wiki/Ottoman_law "Ottoman law") and he was acquitted of murder, although he was ordered to pay compensation to the man's family.[[9]](#cite_note-figes-9) The violent rioting continued for several months and even spread to [Gaza](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City").[[9]](#cite_note-figes-9)

Lyde developed a deranged mental state and had delusions that he was [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist"), [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ") or God himself.[[8]](#cite_note-Tibawi-8)[[9]](#cite_note-figes-9) However, he subsequently recovered sufficiently to write a book on the Alawites, which he completed in [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo") shortly before his death. He died in [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") in Egypt in April 1860.[[1]](#cite_note-Lindemann-1)[[11]](#cite_note-11) He was 35 years old.[[4]](#cite_note-Telegraph-4) He bequeathed his mission at Bhamra to two American missionaries, R. J. Dodds and J. Beattie[[note 1]](#cite_note-13) of the [Reformed Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church_of_North_America "Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America").[[13]](#cite_note-Krieger3-14)

## Publications and influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samuel_Lyde&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Publications and influence")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Alawites_Dance._1880.jpg/330px-Alawites_Dance._1880.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alawites_Dance._1880.jpg)

Alawites dancing the [Dabke](/wiki/Dabke "Dabke") folk dance, 1880

Lyde wrote two books on the Alawites: *The Anseyreeh and Ismaeleeh: A Visit to the Secret Sects of Northern Syria with a View to the Establishment of Schools* (1853) and *The Asian Mystery Illustrated in the History, Religion and Present State of the Ansaireeh or Nusairis of Syria* (1860).[[14]](#cite_note-15) The latter is considered to be a pioneering work, and was the first [monograph](/wiki/Monograph "Monograph") to be written on the Alawite-Nusayri religion.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-Seale-17) It remained the only Western book on the subject until 1900, when [René Dussaud](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Dussaud "René Dussaud") published his *Histoire et religion des Nosairîs*.[[16]](#cite_note-Seale-17)

His description of Alawite doctrines was based on a document called *Kitab al-mashyakha* ("The Manual of the Shaykhs"),[[17]](#cite_note-friedman-18) which he said he had bought from a Christian merchant from Latakia.[[16]](#cite_note-Seale-17) This document appears to have differed in certain respects from other sources on Alawite doctrine.[[17]](#cite_note-friedman-18) For many years it was thought to have been lost and only available through the extracts quoted in translation by Lyde.[[17]](#cite_note-friedman-18) In 2013, it was announced that the document Lyde had used had been discovered in the archives of the Old Library of Jesus College, Cambridge.[[18]](#cite_note-Kriegler1and5-19) Lyde had bequeathed it to his old college, and, apparently, had sent it to Cambridge shortly before his death.[[18]](#cite_note-Kriegler1and5-19)

His writing reveals a negative view of the Alawites and, in particular, he was critical of what he saw as their brigandage, feuds, lying and divorce.[[4]](#cite_note-Telegraph-4) He went as far as saying that "the state of [Alawi] society was a perfect hell upon earth".[[19]](#cite_note-20) *The Asian Mystery* became a popular book and has been described as "colourful" but "unreliable" in some respects.[[4]](#cite_note-Telegraph-4) Nevertheless, Lyde's account remains an influential source on Alawites, and, for instance, is widely quoted on the internet.[[4]](#cite_note-Telegraph-4)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Samuel_Lyde&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-13)** Robert James Dodds and Joseph Beattie had begun their missionary work in Syria in 1856 on behalf of a Reformed Presbyterian denomination known as the Reformed Presbyterian Church Old Light Synod.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
